coredata.dc:title: Carbon dots nanofluid: Reducing injection pressure in unconventional reservoir by regulating oil/water/rock interfacial properties
coredata.dc:creator: Cao, Mengjiao
Li, Yuan
Song, Xuguang
Chen, Yesheng
Sun, Linghui
Shi, Xin
Dai, Caili
Yuan, Bin
coredata.dc:description: Effective development of unconventional resources is impeded by water flooding-related challenges, such as high pressure and under-injection, which hinder the replenishment of formation energy. Nanofluids have shown promise in reducing injection pressure, but traditional nanoparticles (NPs) face limitations in unconventional reservoirs due to their unsuitable size for micro-nano pores and aggregation tendencies. In this paper, novel carbon dots (CDs) with a simple, environmentally benign, and controllable synthetic approach have been developed, and their pressure-reduction performance and mechanism have been systematically explored. The particle size of the CDs could be tailored by a well-timely quenching reaction. CDs nanofluid demonstrated superior stability, dispersibility, and temperature/salinity resistance. In addition, CDs can regulate interfacial properties, including oil/rock and oil/water interfaces. It was found that CDs could be partially absorbed onto pore walls, reducing oil adhesion sites by 39%, altering wettability from oil-wet to neutral, and reducing rock surface roughness by 29.25%. Furthermore, CDs were rapidly adsorbed onto the remaining oil film in the pore space, reducing interfacial tension while solidifying the oil surfaces, increasing shear force around the oil surface by 1.7 times during subsequent water scouring, which makes the oil film break up more readily and remain smaller in size to migrate in the channeling. The modulation of the interface properties by CDs improved the stripping efficiency of the attached oil film and ameliorated the homogeneity and smoothness of flow channel, resulting in a significant reduction in injection pressure. Core displacement tests showed that a 0.1 wt% CDs nanofluid could achieve a pressure reduction rate of 35%. The developed CDs nanofluid represents a promising solution for reducing injection pressure and improving oil recovery in unconventional reservoirs.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Nano-technologies
Carbon dots
Pressure reduction
Interface properties regulation
Oil film stripping

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Measurement of wettability and roughness of rock surface
Dispersion and stability of CDs nanofluid
Regulation of pore throat surface wettability
Regulation of pore throat surface roughness

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) Schematic illustration of the synthesis of CDs nanofluid, (b) the photos of the products under different reaction conditions, and (c) the average particle size of CDs at different reaction temperatures and times.

(a)TEM image of CDs, (b) AFM height distribution of CDs, (c)FTIR spectra of CDs, (d)XPS survey scans of CDs survey spectrum showing C(1 s), O(1 s), N(1 s) and Si(2p) corelevels, and (e) fitted high-resolution spectra of C(1 s), N(1 s) and Si(2p) regions.
Effect of (a) concentrations, (b) temperature, and (c) salinity on size and zeta potential of CDs in aqueous solution.
Pressure reduction performance of different solutions in brine-saturated cores.
Pressure reduction performance of CDs (0.1%) in oil saturated core.

(a) Change of oil droplet contact angle with time (0,300,1300 min) in CDs nanofluid. Adhesion distribution of rock surface before(b) and after(c) adsorption of CDs. Frequency distribution histogram and Gaussian fitting of the adhesion before(d) and after (e) adsorption of CDs.
Change of root mean square (RMS) roughness of pore wall before and after CDs adsorption.
(a)Interfacial tension and (b)interfacial modulus curves of CDs.
Shear force filed around the oil droplets before(a) and after(b) adsorption of CDs. The left/right side shows the channel wall and the oil–water interface.
Schematic diagram of pressure reduction mechanism of CDs.
The effect of CDs concentration on its pressure reduction performance.
The effect of core permeability on CDs nanofluid’s pressure reduction performance.

head.ce:title: Carbon dots nanofluid: Reducing injection pressure in unconventional reservoir by regulating oil/water/rock interfacial properties

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Effective development of unconventional resources is impeded by water flooding-related challenges, such as high pressure and under-injection, which hinder the replenishment of formation energy. Nanofluids have shown promise in reducing injection pressure, but traditional nanoparticles (NPs) face limitations in unconventional reservoirs due to their unsuitable size for micro-nano pores and aggregation tendencies. In this paper, novel carbon dots (CDs) with a simple, environmentally benign, and controllable synthetic approach have been developed, and their pressure-reduction performance and mechanism have been systematically explored. The particle size of the CDs could be tailored by a well-timely quenching reaction. CDs nanofluid demonstrated superior stability, dispersibility, and temperature/salinity resistance. In addition, CDs can regulate interfacial properties, including oil/rock and oil/water interfaces. It was found that CDs could be partially absorbed onto pore walls, reducing oil adhesion sites by 39%, altering wettability from oil-wet to neutral, and reducing rock surface roughness by 29.25%. Furthermore, CDs were rapidly adsorbed onto the remaining oil film in the pore space, reducing interfacial tension while solidifying the oil surfaces, increasing shear force around the oil surface by 1.7 times during subsequent water scouring, which makes the oil film break up more readily and remain smaller in size to migrate in the channeling. The modulation of the interface properties by CDs improved the stripping efficiency of the attached oil film and ameliorated the homogeneity and smoothness of flow channel, resulting in a significant reduction in injection pressure. Core displacement tests showed that a 0.1 wt% CDs nanofluid could achieve a pressure reduction rate of 35%. The developed CDs nanofluid represents a promising solution for reducing injection pressure and improving oil recovery in unconventional reservoirs.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: As the demand for crude oil grows, unconventional oil and gas resources have become increasingly important. However, unconventional reservoirs such as tight oil, low permeability, and extra-low oil reservoirs are featured poor physical properties, poor pore connectivity, fine pore throats(micro-nano-scale), and strong heterogeneity [1–3]. Such leads to rapid production decline, low productivity and low recovery rate(e.g., tight oil recovery is only 5–10%) if only relying on natural energy extraction. Thus, low-cost and simple process water injection development is often performed to complement production energy and enhance oil recovery [4,5]. However, the extremely fine pore throat units and poor connectivity in unconventional reservoirs lead to high flow resistance [6]. Besides, the fine pore throats have a large specific surface area, allowing a much higher percentage of crude oil in direct contact with and bound by the rock wall than conventional reservoirs, further reducing the effective permeability and exacerbating the pore disconnectivity. As a result, the pressure rises sharply, and even the injected water is hard to migrate during water injection, eventually leading to severe under-injection, and the formation energy cannot be effectively supplemented [7,8]. Therefore, an adequate pressure reduction technique is urgently needed to reduce water injection pressure and ensure the injection volume to enhance the oil recovery (EOR) of unconventional reservoirs.

In recent years, nanomaterials such as nano-silica, nano-titanium dioxide and nano-alumina have been widely studied and applied in oilfield development [9–15]. With a small size and large specific surface area, nanomaterials can enter porous media and adsorb on the rock surface, thus reducing water injection pressure and increasing the injection rate, which ultimately plays a role in lowering fluid flow resistance and enhancing oil recovery [11,12]. It has been reported that nanoparticles(NPs) can alter the rock wettability by competitive adsorption between NPs and water film on the rock surface, resulting in a slip effect that widens the water flow channel. Assef et al. has considered that nanofluid can inhibit the effect of clay particle migration, improving the outcome of low salinity/alkaline water flooding and lowering the water flow resistance to some extent [13].Beyond that, the amount of oil phase in pores and rock surface impacted the reservoir’s relative permeability, residual oil distribution and capillary pressure, which inevitably changed the oil/water flow state and thus affected the water injection pressure. More recent but limited studies have demonstrated that NPs could improve oil displacement efficiency via some different mechanisms, mainly including changes in liquid–liquid and solid–liquid interfaces properties: interfacial tension reduction, which increases oil/water mobility [14,15]; wettability alteration, which allows lower adhesion energy between oil and rock surface; and increased structural disjoining pressure, which makes the oil to be readily detached from the rock surface [16–19]. Specifically, NPs can spontaneously form ordered structures in wedge-shaped oil/water/solid contact regions where oil films can be separated from solid surfaces by applied stresses (disjoining structural pressures). Despite these advances in nanomaterials, the practical application of traditional nanomaterials (nano-SiO2) in unconventional reservoirs is still hampered. The main reason is that the average diameter of rock pores of the unconventional reservoir is too small(micro-nano scale) to allow traditional NPs (e.g., nano-SiO2 typically larger than 15 nm)to pass freely and play their own roles [20,21]. Considering a clogging process of a pore channel with NPs whose dimension is smaller than the pore channel, experiments have shown that if NPs account for more than 10% of pore size, they would readily block the pore channel [22]. Further, when the dispersion of NPs in nanofluids is unstable, it is difficult for them to display the designated surface functionality at the oil/water/rock interfaces. It usually leads to aggregated sedimentation or plugging of pore throat units [2,23]. Therefore, developing smaller-sized, self-dispersed nanomaterials with interfacial modulation capabilities that can enter and function stably in nano-micro pores is essential to avoid such problems.

Quantum dots (QDs), a nanomaterial with 3D dimensions all below 10 nm, have flourished in several fields such as photocatalysis, sensors, and medical imaging due to their biocompatibility, excellent electron transfer capability, strong fluorescence, chemical and photostability [24–27]. They have a homogeneous or core–shell structure, and their physical and chemical properties, such as size, composition, and surface features, are determined by the precursors, ph environment, and reaction conditions [28]. Structurally, QDs are accompanied by a large number of surface defects and these may lead to poor stability and lower yields. Regarding the surface chemistry, yield and stability of QDs, a common strategy is surface passivation or functionalization for their optimized structure [29]. Based on this, the ultra-small size (matching with nanopores) and diverse surface functional groups (easier modification or functionalization) render QDs intriguing and have unique potential in developing unconventional reservoirs [26,27]. For the past couple of years, some researchers have studied oil displacement and imbibition performance of carbon/silicon based-quantum dots. For instance, Ioan-AlexandruBaragau et al. prepared S-functionalized carbon quantum dots (S-CQDs) via continuous hydrothermal flow synthesis, which enhanced oil recovery of 17% by altering the wettability of carbonate rocks toward water wet and caused temporary log-jamming [30]. Zhou et al. reported a nanofluid prepared by ultra-small silicon quantum dots coated with a zwitterionic surfactant, which showed good stability in synthetic high divalent salinity water at high temperature and recovered 25.72 original oil-in-place from Bakken core samples by imbibition [12]. Zhou et al. synthesized silicon dots by the hydrothermal method and grafted sodium p-aminobenzene sulfonate onto their surface to obtain active Si-dots fluid with imbibition recovery of 38% in ultra-low permeability cores [31]. Our group prepared a hydrophobic carbon dots nanofluid dispersed by Tween-80, which has a sound pressure reduction performance of up to 17% in tight reservoirs [26,32]. Nevertheless, the complex synthesis process, uncontrollable degree of reaction, indispensable surface modification, and limited understanding of the mechanism hindered the further application of QDs in reducing water injection pressure [33].

This study aims to synthesize carbon dots (CDs, carbon based QDs) with facile, process-controllability and good water dispersity to reduce water injection pressure in the unconventional reservoir. CDs were produced from glucose and 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane via a one-step hydrothermal method, and the particle size was controlled by pH adjustment. The prepared CDs exhibited excellent stability and hydrophilicity, enabling their stable dispersion in aqueous solutions. The stability, interface regulation properties (including oil–water and rock-liquid interfaces), and pressure reduction performance of the CDs nanofluids were systematically investigated. Innovatively, the mechanism of pressure reduction and oil droplet stripping of CDs nanofluids at the pore scale was studied using an atomic force microscope (AFM) and microscopic visualization technologies. This research provides valuable insights into addressing the challenges of high-water pressure and under-injection in unconventional reservoir development and significantly enhancing oil recovery.

3-Aminopropyltriethoxysilane (APTES, 97%) and D-glucose (≥99.5%) were purchased from Shanghai Aladdin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. Hydrochloric acid (HCl) and sodium chloride (NaCl) were procured from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd and used to adjust the solution pH and salinity respectively. Crude oil was obtained from the Xinjiang oilfield, and the mixture of crude oil and kerosene (0.82 g/ cm3) was used as simulated formation oil. Natural outcrop cores (∅=2.5 cm) were taken from extra-low permeability or tight oil reservoirs in Changqing Oilfield (China). All the experiments were performed using ultrapure water (18.2 MΩ.cm).

CDs nanofluid was synthesized with D-glucose and APTES as sources at a specific temperature. In a typical synthesis, 10 g of D-glucose was dissolved in 100 mL of ultrapure water, and then 10 mL APTES was added at a rate of 0.5 mL/min with stirring. Stir at 40℃, 50℃, 55℃ and 60℃ for 2 ∼ 24 h respectively until the colorless solution turns dark brown. After that, hydrochloric acid was dropped to adjust the pH of the solution to 7 to stop the reaction and obtain CDs nanofluid with good dispersibility.

The morphology and microstructure of CDs were detected by transmission electron microscope (TEM, JEOL-2100F). The size/height distribution of particles was measured by atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker, German). The hydrodynamic diameters and zeta potential of CDs with different concentrations, salinity, and temperature were measured by dynamic lighting scattering (DLS, NanoBrook Omni, Brookhaven Instruments Corporation) to evaluate the stability of nanofluids. The surface functional groups of CDs were determined by Fourier-Transform Spectroscopy (FT-IR) using a NICOLET 6700 (Thermo Fisher, USA.) spectrometer. X-Ray Photoelectron Spectroscopy (XPS) was measured using a K-Alpha (Thermo fisher, USA.) photoelectron spectrometer.

The pressure reduction and enhanced oil recovery performance of different chemicals were tested in unsaturated/ saturated oil cores (permeability 0.17 ∼ 5.05mD, porosity 10.67 ∼ 16.91%). First, cores were cleaned and dried at 60℃ for 48 h. Subsequently, the dried cores were saturated with simulated oil or brine. Cores saturated with oil were aged for 7 days at 60℃. Then, core flooding tests were performed as follows:
(1)
The saturated core was put into the core holder, and 2 MPa of confining pressure was applied. (The injection pressure was ∼ 0 MPa at this point, and the confining pressure was always ∼ 2 MPa higher than injection pressure)
(2)
The saturated core was injected with brine at a flow rate of 0.2 mL/min. The pressure drop between the inlet and outlet was monitored in real-time until it stabilized, at which time the platform value P1 was recorded. During this period, the oil output volume of saturated oil cores was recorded in time.
(3)
After releasing the pressure, a certain volume of chemical agent was injected into the core.
(4)
After standing for 24 h, the pressure was re-released and the brine was re-injected. Platform value P2 was recorded (oil output volume was recorded in real-time during saturated oil core testing).

As a result, the pressure reduction rate S was calculated as [26,32]:
S =
P 1
-
P 2
P 1
× 100 %
.The core displacement tests device is shown in Fig.S1.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Measurement of wettability and roughness of rock surface
ce:section.ce:para: The contact angle was measured by JC2000D2 contact angle measurement (Zhongchen Digital Technic Apparatus Corporation). In this experiment, the core sheet saturated with simulated oil and aged for 7 days (60℃) was placed into a nanofluid, and a drop of simulated oil was placed on the bottom surface of the core to record the change of contact angle of oil droplets with time.

Atomic force microscope (AFM) hydrophobic tip was used to detect the change of adhesion force on the core surface before/after CDs nanofluid treatment to reveal the alteration of microscopic wettability. In this experiment, the cores were processed as slices (∅=10, h = 1 mm) to serve as substrates. First, the AFM tip was hydrophobized in 10 mM dodecyl mercaptan-anhydrous ethanol solution for 12 h. Then the hydrophobic tip was used to scan a 1 × 1 μm area on the core surface (matching micro-nano pore size), and the 2-D adhesion force distribution map (32 × 32 pixel) and micro-morphology map were obtained. Subsequently, the core slice was immersed in CDs nanofluid for 12 h and repeated the above methods to obtain the adhesion force distribution and micro-morphology alteration after particles adsorption [34].

The interfacial tension and modulus were measured using Tracker Automatic Tensiometer (TECLIS Interface Technology Co., Ltd). The sample was placed in the sample tank; the needle generated the oil phase under the liquid surface. Morphological images of the droplet were collected and analyzed by the camera module while the droplet profile changed to calculate volume, surface area and surface tension. Then droplet was subjected to area sinusoidal oscillation to determine the interfacial tension response value to obtain the viscoelastic modulus. In addition, the visualized flow field and a shear force field around the oil droplet were quantitatively measured by micro-particle image velocimetry (µPIV, LaVision. Ltd) to analyze the state of the oil droplet in the pore-throat units intuitively [33]. The flow visualization device and microfluidic device diagram are shown in Fig.S2.

The reaction kinetics were systematically investigated to explore the influence of reaction temperature and time on the size of CDs. CDs were prepared at 40℃, 50℃, 55℃ and 60 ℃ respectively, and the reaction was stopped after different reaction times. The schematic illustration of the synthesis steps of CDs is shown in Fig. 1 (a). Under different reaction temperatures of 40,50,55 and 60℃, the size of CDs gradually increased with reaction time, along with the solution color transition from transparent to light yellow and brown (Fig. 1b). In addition, the reaction rate increased with increasing reaction temperature. Slow kinetics was observed at lower temperatures of 40 and 50℃. And the reaction process was significantly intensified at higher temperatures of 55-60℃, with particle sizes exceeding 10 nm within 6 h at 55℃ (Fig. 1c). When the reaction was carried out at 60 ℃ for more than 3 h, the products precipitated. This is because excessive temperature or long reaction time intensified the thermal movement of CDs, and the collision probability between particles increased significantly, so as to severe cross-linking, aggregation, and deposition of particles. Therefore, it is necessary to accurately control the reaction conditions and select the appropriate time to quench the reaction. It can be seen from the experimental results that the particle size can be precisely regulated by altering the reaction temperature and time and controlling the solution conditions (pH = 7) to quench the reaction in time. The reaction stopped when the pH value was adjusted to neutral, and the product has good static placing stability. The particle size does not increase within 30 days under specific experimental conditions. (Supporting information 2.1).

Unconventional reservoirs have abundant micro-nano-scale pore throats, so the injected NPs need to be small enough to ensure injectability. In addition, the type and quantities of functional groups of the particles significantly impact their interaction with interfaces, their dispersion stability and the difficulty of surface modification, which directly determines the application performance. The CDs (take the product synthesized at 50℃ for 6 h as the sample) were analyzed using Transmission Electron Microscopy(TEM) and Atomic Force Microscopy(AFM) to ascertain the dimensions of the particles, FT-IR spectroscopy to determine their functionalities and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy(XPS) to determine the surface chemistry and elemental composition.

TEM image (Fig. 2 a) shows that the CDs are spherical and well dispersed, with a size mainly distributed at about 2.7 nm. AFM was used to scan the 1 μm2 area of mica surface treated with low-concentration aqueous dispersion of CDs, and the 2-D height distribution and microstructure of particles were characterized. It can be seen from Fig. 2b that the particles are evenly dispersed, without apparent agglomeration, the section height of particles is concentrated in the range of 2.1–3.1 nm. According to statistics, the average size of CDs is about 2.7 nm, and the variance size is 0.13, which is in agreement with the morphology measured by TEM. The experimental results show that the synthesized CDs have small sizes and high uniformity, thus allowing good injectivity into micro-nano pores [26].

The FT-IR spectroscopy (Fig. 2c) reveals the composition of the functional groups on the surface of CDs. It can be seen that CDs exhibit a variety of oxygen functionalities (carbonyl, carboxyl or hydroxyl) and nitrogen functional groups (amino) on their surface. Such a cocktail of oxygen and nitrogen functionalities lends excellent solubility in water of the particles. Specifically, a broad peak (3431 cm−1) can be ascribed to the vibration of O-H (R-OH, –COOH), and the peak of 2900 cm−1 can be ascribed to the C-H stretching vibrations. The carbonyl vibrations (C = O) can be assigned to stretch at 1633 cm−1, and C-O-H vibration may be assigned to stretch at 1429 cm−1. Moreover, the Si-O-Si peak observed at 1045 cm-1 implies the hydrolysis and condensation of APTES. It should be noted that the bending vibration at 1095 cm−1 reveals the existence of –NH2. In contrast to the most widely reported nano-SiO2, which only has silicon hydroxyl groups on the surface, the variety of active sites on the surface of CDs makes modification simple and diverse. Meanwhile, much hydrophilic oxygen and nitrogen-containing functional groups significantly contribute to their dispersion and stability in aqueous [26].

The XPS spectra (Fig. 2d-Fig. 2e) lend further support to the FT-IR spectroscopy analysis, indicating the presence of carbon, oxygen, nitrogen and silicon atoms on the surface of CDs. In the expanded XPS spectra, the C1s peaks at 283.6 eV, 284.7 eV, and 285.8 eV can be assigned to C-Si, C-C/C = C, C-OH/C-NH2 respectively. Further analysis of the N1s XPS spectrum reveals the presence of C-NH2 and N-(C)3 at 398.8 eV and 400.2 eV, respectively. Also, the Si 2p peaks at 101.0 eV and 102.5 eV, which can be assigned to Si-C and Si-O. The XPS spectra are in agreement with FT-IR spectroscopy analysis for CDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Dispersion and stability of CDs nanofluid
ce:section.ce:para: Dispersion stability is a prerequisite for the NPs in the bulk phase to play a long-term role in the formation conditions. Here, the effects of concentration, temperature, and ionic strength on the hydrodynamic particle diameter and zeta potential of the CDs in the aqueous solution were tested. The results are shown in Fig. 3 .

It can be seen that the hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential of CDs fluctuated slightly within the concentration of 0.01–0.2 wt%. The particle size is in the range of 3–4.5 nm and zeta potential range of −30 ∼ -40 mV. This is because the particle spacing decreases with the increased concentration, increasing the probability of particle-to-particle contact, which affects the surface bilayer. However, the surface of CDs contains many hydrophilic groups, making them well dispersed in the experimental concentration range [36]. Similarly, with the temperature rising from 30 °C to 90 °C, the particle size fluctuates slightly but not much. This indicates that the nanofluid has good temperature resistance. The effect of ionic strength on the stability of nanofluid is more prominent. With the increase of ionic strength, the particle size and zeta potential show a slow and fast-increasing trend. This is because the Brownian motion of anions and cations in saline destroyed the electrostatic forces between the CDs, which leads to a decrease in the absolute value of zeta potential of CDs. At the same time, the decreased electrostatic repulsion between the particles causes sedimentation or aggregation, resulting in a gradual increase in the measured average particle size [37]. The experimental results show that when the NaCl concentration is less than 8 %, the particle size is less than 15 nm, while when NaCl concentration reaches 10 %, the particle size in the nanofluid increases significantly but is still smaller than 100 nm, indicating that it has a certain salinity resistance.

To probe into the injectability, compatibility, and impact on water injection pressure and oil recovery of CDs to the extra-low-permeability reservoir. The pressure reduction performance of CDs nanofluids(0.1%), conventional unmodified-SiO2 nanofluids(0.1%), and surfactants solution (sodium dodecyl sulfonate, SDS,0.1%) in the brine saturated cores (0.25 mD)were compared first (Fig. 4 ). The experiment is divided into three stages: initial water flooding, chemical flooding, and subsequent water flooding. It was found that the stable pressure of initial water flooding is ∼ 1.1 MPa due to the same permeability and injection rate, while the stable pressure of subsequent water flooding changes significantly after injecting different solutions. CDs nanofluid has good pressure reduction performance, which decreases the injection pressure by 22.4%. SDS solution reduces the injection pressure by 10.9%. In contrast, SiO2 nanofluid causes the pressure to rise instead of drop, increasing the injection pressure by 41.7%. This may be because SDS solution has a limited modification of the rock surface and is prone to lose efficacy after water flooding. Hence, its performance of pressure reduction has certain limitations [38]. Nano-SiO2 is large and has poor inhomogeneity, which does not match micro-nano pore-throat units. Besides, it is easy to agglomerate and block the pore throat units, resulting in higher injection pressure [26]. In comparison, CDs, on the one hand, have a smaller size of 2.7 nm, which may be better for migration in micro-nano pores without blocking the pores and channels. On the other hand, CDs have certain interface activity, which may be adsorbed on oil films and rocks appropriately to regulate the interfacial properties and play a better role in the extra-low-permeability reservoir.

The concentration of CDs nanofluid has an essential effect on the pressure reduction performance of the reservoirs. The injection pressure of CDs nanofluids with different concentrations(0.01%-0.2%) varies with the injection volumes, as shown in Table 1 . It can be seen that CDs nanofluids of 0.01–0.2% concentration all have the ability to reduce the injection pressure. With the concentration increasing from 0.01 wt% to 0.10 wt%, the pressure reduction performance improves, and the maximum pressure reduction rate reaches 22.8% at 0.1 wt%. But exceeding the optimal concentration will lead to severe permeability damage. There is little change with the concentration increased from 0.1 wt% to 0.15 wt%. While as the concentration continues to grow, the pressure reduction rate decreases, reaching 17.5% at 0.20 wt%. This is probably because the higher nanofluid concentration leads to extra particles in the pore throat. The extra particles block some fine pore throat structure during the subsequent water flooding, resulting in the decrease of the effective channel, which eventually leads to the deterioration of its pressure reduction performance [26].

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of pore throat surface wettability
ce:section.ce:para:
Considering the properties of the rock surface, mainly including wettability and roughness, which directly affect the oil/water flow state in the reservoir [39,40], the effect of CDs nanofluid on the variation of the rock surface properties is investigated [16–18]. The dynamic contact angle of an oil droplet on saturated oil core pieces in 0.1 wt% nanofluids is shown in Fig. 6 (a). The results showed that the contact angle increased continuously after adding CDs, reaching ∼ 83° from the initial 50° at 300 min, and then continued to rise to a maximum at 1300 min (∼104°), which reached an equilibrium state. This indicates that the CDs has good spreadability in the oil/water/solid three-phase contact line and can adjust the wettability of the oil-wet rock to neutral. The effect of wettability alteration on pressure reduction can be explained by the change of adhesion energy between oil droplets and rock surface. According to the adhesion energy formula
W
adhesion
= σ
1 + cos [ 03 B 8 ]
(
σ
is interfacial tension,
θ
is the contact angle between oil and rock surface), the adsorption of CDs on the rock surface increased the contact angle, thus significantly reducing the adhesion energy. This lead to the low flow resistance of water, which is beneficial for reducing the injection pressure. In addition, the change in contact angle may also be due to the adsorption of CDs at the oil/water and solid/liquid interfaces. A mass of particles accumulates at the three-phase contact area, resulting in structural disjoining pressure, which shows the trend of stripping the oil droplets [19]. The macroscopic manifestation is a change in contact angle.

Contact angle closely depends on parameters such as roughness and chemical inhomogeneity of the core surface. It is based on macroscopic statistical averages within a particular sample area and cannot reflect the hydrophilic and hydrophobic distribution at micro-nano scale of the pore [34,41]. Therefore, the AFM was used to explore the pore wall's micro-region (1 μm × 1 μm), and surface adhesion distributions of the core before/after CDs adsorption were obtained to quantify the alteration of micro wettability. It is well known that the magnitude of adhesion force is related to surface interactions, mainly including van der Waals(VDW) interactions, double layer (EDL) interactions, and possibly hydrophobic interactions [42]. In the present study, the contribution of VDW and EDL interactions to the adhesion force distribution in the aqueous environment does not change significantly before and after CDs adsorption. So changes in the adhesion force distribution on the core surface are considered the results of hydrophobic forces. The adhesion of hydrophobic AFM tips to hydrophobic regions is much stronger than hydrophilic regions, so the distribution of hydrophilic and hydrophobic micro-zone can be determined by comparing the adhesion strength. As shown in Fig. 6(b-c), the bright and dark areas represent the robust and weak adhesion regions, which correspond to the relatively hydrophobic and hydrophilic regions. Comparing Fig. 6. (b) and (c), it can be seen that the bright and dark areas in the adhesion distribution map before nanofluid treatment are uneven. This indicates that the hydrophile micro-zone on the initial rock surface is heterogeneous, which is caused by the complex structure of the pore surface, including rough structure, mineral diversity, and mineral distribution unevenly [43,44]. While after the adsorption of CDs, the color of the adhesion distribution becomes darker and more uniform, indicating that the adhesion in each micro-zone became weak. This is due to the uniform adsorption of particles on the rock surface, which forms a denser particle film and homogenizes the overall wettability. Notably, the histogram of the frequency distribution and Gaussian fitting of adhesion quantitatively verified the above phenomenon. The initial core adhesive force is mainly distributed between 0 and 16.8 nN(Fig. 6d) with a broad range, and the peak of the fit is concentrated ∼ 7.53 nN. In contrast, the distribution of adhesion after particle adsorption becomes significantly narrower(Fig. 6e), and the distribution range becomes ∼ 0–6.87 nN with a Gaussian-fitted peak of 2.87 nN. This is more indicative of the alteration in the pore wall properties, where the particles regulate the pore surface, the microscopic wettability from non-homogeneous to homogeneous and from hydrophobic to relatively hydrophilic [45,46].

The adhesion force results measured by AFM match the macroscopic contact angle results. The wettability of the rock surface is changed after CDs adsorption. The microscopic wettability homogeneity is enhanced by about 2.45 times, the adhesion between the oil phase and the rock pore wall is reduced by about 39%, resulting in a significant reduction of the strong adhesion sites on the rock pore wall. As a result, the oil film adhered in the pore channel is more easily stripped down from the rock surface, which increases the reservoir water-phase permeability and broadens the effective pore radius.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Regulation of pore throat surface roughness
ce:section.ce:para: The micro-nano scale roughness affects the solid–liquid boundary's flow behavior, directly impacting the injection pressure. Pore walls of rock are non-homogeneous, irregular, and complex, and the modulation of the microscopic morphology of pore walls by NPs adsorption will inevitably change the flow behavior of fluids in the solid–liquid boundary layer [47]. To explore the effect of CDs on the wall surface's morphology more intuitively and accurately, pore walls before/after nanofluid treatment were scanned using AFM. Twenty-micrometer areas on the pore walls before/after CDs adsorption were measured separately. Each region's root-mean-square (RMS) roughness was counted, as shown in Fig. 7 . The statistical results show that the adsorption of CDs reduced the surface’s RMS roughness significantly. The average RMS of the initial wall surface is ∼ 44.8 nm and decreases to 31.7 nm after particles adsorption. The average reduction rate of roughness is about 29.24%. This is because the wall surface with a high degree of non-homogeneity and roughness is partially adsorbed by CDs after the nanofluid scouring, filling the surface unevenness and reducing the surface RMS. It is well-known that when the pore wall surface is rougher, the fluid flow with higher energy loss leads to a higher fluid resistance coefficient, which results in higher flow resistance. However, partial adsorption of CDs regulates the wall roughness, makes the channels smoother, and the injection pressure drastically reduced [48].

The micro-nano pores in unconventional reservoirs have a large amount of residual oil film that adheres to the wall during water flooding, which significantly reduces the effective porosity of the matrix and leads to high injection pressure. Interfacial tension (IFT) between oil and water plays a vital role in injection pressure reduction and oil recovery as it reflects the oil and water migration capacity to a certain extent [49]. While the viscoelasticity modulus reflects the mechanical strength of the oil–water interface, which will affect the stress state and flow behavior of the oil droplet. To investigate the influence of CDs on the oil–water interfacial rheological properties, the IFT and viscoelastic modulus between oil phase (dodecane) and CDs nanofluids with different concentrations (0.01–0.20 wt%) were measured, as shown in Fig. 8 . The experimental results showed that the IFT decreased rapidly, slowly with time, and finally stabilized. The IFT decreased with the increase in nanofluid concentration as well. The adsorption of CDs at the oil–water interface reduced the IFT from ∼ 30 mN/m to ∼ 17 mN/m. Meanwhile, the interfacial viscoelasticity tends to increase rapidly, slowly and finally reach equilibrium with time. With the increase of nanofluid concentration, the interfacial viscoelasticity modulus also increased. CDs adsorbed at the oil–water interface can increase the modulus from ∼ 43 mN/m to ∼ 88 mN/m. This indicates that the prepared CDs have certain interfacial activity and can be adsorbed at the oil–water interface to form a dense network structure, regulating the interfacial properties to a certain extent [50–52]. Specifically, this may be because the reaction sources or precursors make the products contain heteroatoms (Si, N, etc.), conferring relatively hydrophobic microregions on the product's surface. While a large number of oxygen/nitrogen-containing functional groups on the surface allowed hydrophilicity to the CDs so that they could be well dispersed in aqueous. Such structure makes the CDs somewhat amphiphilic and can be adsorbed at the oil–water interface.

The modulation of oil–water interface properties by CDs will inevitably affect the stress states around the residual oil during the flooding process, leading to changes in oil film morphology and its flow behavior [35,48]. Therefore, to understand the effect of CDs adsorption on the remaining oil film stripping, the shear stress field around the oil droplets in different fluids in the visualized chip was measured by microparticle image velocimeter (μ-PIV); the results are shown in Fig. 9 .

By comparison, it was found that the shear stress field around the oil droplet in water or nanofluid follows a decreasing trend and then increases from left to right. The shear stresses in both systems are almost the same near the wall, with the largest shear stress; the shear stress is the smallest at the midpoint between the channel wall and the oil droplet surface, while there is a significant difference around the oil droplet in the two systems. The shear stress around oil droplet in CDs nanofluid is ∼ 1.72 times higher than that of the pure water system. The particle has interfacial activity and can be adsorbed on the oil droplet surface, forming a denser film and solidifying the surface [35,52]. The hardened shell changes the flow field around the oil droplet during scouring, generating tremendous shear stress. The increased shear force causes the oil droplets easily break up, and the broken tiny oil droplets will not be easy to secondary adhesion with the wall due to the CDs adsorption. On the other hand, they are also not accessible to coalescence with other oil droplets, so they will remain smaller and be carried out of the formation with a lower flow resistance [53,54].

Based on the study of CDs’ regulation of rock-water and oil–water interfaces, the pressure reduction mechanism of CDs is summarized in Fig. 10 . The modulation of the interface (oil–water/solid–liquid interface) by CDs, results in the shear force enhancement on the droplet, wettability alteration and roughness reduction of the pore wall, and the structural disjoining pressure caused by particles diffusion are significant to the pressure reduction performance. During first water flooding, the pore throat wall is rough, and the oil film with its strong adhesion exists due to the tiny pore throat and oil-wet state, resulting in high injection pressure. After injecting CDs nanofluid, the particles adsorb at the solid–liquid interface, modulating the non-homogeneous wall surface to homogeneous and altering it from oil-wet to neutral wetting. This significantly reduces the oil adhesion force and the number of strong adhesion sites of the wall. In addition, tiny pores were filled with particles, which significantly reduced pore roughness. Meanwhile, the CDs form a wedge structure in the oil/water/solid three-phase contact zone due to the large diffusion force, and the wedge-shaped structure creates a forward thrust to peel off the oil droplets further.

On the other hand, CDs adsorb at the oil–water interface simultaneously to solidify the interface. Such interface is subjected to stronger shear forces as the water rushes through, which works in concert with the structural disjoining pressure effect to make the oil droplets more accessible to be stripped from the wall. At the same time, the solidified oil droplets are more likely to break up when passing through the pore throat units and can be maintained at a smaller size without secondary adhesion to the wall. During the subsequent water flooding, the fine droplets are carried out with low resistance in smoother channels, achieving enhanced recovery and pressure reduction.

In this study, we developed a simple and controlled method to fabricate CDs to reduce injection pressure in unconventional reservoirs. CDs nanofluid demonstrated superior stability, dispersibility, and certain anti-temperature/anti-salinity properties. The size distribution of CDs was maintained at 3–4.5 nm under certain temperatures and salt conditions in aqueous, with a zeta potential of −30 to −40 mV. The results showed that CDs could effectively regulate the oil/rock interface properties. They partially adsorbed onto the pore walls, resulting in a 39% reduction in the adhesion sites on the wall and altering the wettability from oil-wet to neutral, reducing the residual oil film in the pores widened the flow channel. The CDs also reduced the roughness of the pore surface, resulting in a 29.24% reduction in RMS and a smoother channel. Furthermore, CDs could be partially adsorbed at the oil/water interface to adjust interfacial properties, leading to a 43% reduction in interfacial tension and a 105% promotion in the viscoelastic modulus. At the same time, they solidified the oil droplet surface, resulting in a 1.7-fold increase in shear force around the oil surface during scouring, making oil droplets more likely to break up and remain smaller to migrate. Notably, the CDs nanofluid delivered a prominent pressure reduction rate of ∼ 35% without any surfactant addition, providing a feasible solution for synthesizing versatile surfactant-free nanomaterials to reduce injection pressure and enhance oil recovery in extra-low permeability/tight oil reservoirs.

Mengjiao Cao: Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Formal analysis, Conceptualization. Yuan Li: Methodology, Data curation. Xuguang Song: Writing – review & editing, Formal analysis. Yesheng Chen: Data curation. Linghui Sun: Investigation. Xin Shi: Writing – review & editing. Caili Dai: Validation, Supervision, Resources, Funding acquisition. Bin Yuan: Writing – review & editing, Visualization, Funding acquisition, Conceptualization.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The work was supported by the National Key R&D Program of China (NO. 2022YFE0129900), National Key Research and Development Program of China (2019YFA0708700), the Innovation fund project for graduate student of China University of Petroleum (East China) (No. 22CX04050A), and the Fundamental Research Funds for the Central Universities.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.fuel.2023.129046.

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
